text.title: Backlog Principles: DEEP, INVEST, Bucket Definitions, and Item Quality
text.summary: Core rules for a healthy backlog: structural principles, the four-bucket model, and the minimum quality bar for a Ready item.

rule.statement: Apply DEEP to every backlog: Detailed (top-of-backlog items have AC and estimates), Emergent (bottom items stay rough), Estimated (all Ready items are sized), Prioritized (every item has an explicit rank).
rule.rationale: Treating bottom-of-backlog items with the same detail as top items wastes refinement time. DEEP matches detail investment to delivery probability.

rule.statement: Every Ready item must satisfy INVEST: Independent (no hidden dependencies), Negotiable, Valuable (explicit user benefit), Estimable, Small (fits in one sprint), Testable (has Given/When/Then AC).
rule.rationale: Items that fail INVEST become sprint killers — they block other work (not Independent), cannot be completed in one cycle (not Small), or fail review (not Testable).

antipattern.description: Ready item has no acceptance criteria, or AC is a restatement of the title.
antipattern.reason: Untestable AC makes sprint planning slow and definition-of-done review impossible.

rule.statement: Maintain exactly four buckets: Ready (next 1-2 sprints, fully refined), Upcoming (1-3 sprints, defined with some detail), Backlog (future, rough ideas), Icebox (parked, low probability). Enforce explicit promotion criteria between buckets.
rule.rationale: Without defined buckets and promotion criteria, all items look equal and planning degrades to whoever speaks loudest.

rule.statement: Cap Ready at 2 sprints of team capacity (~8-25 items). Below 8 means insufficient pipeline; above 25 means refinement is being wasted on items that may never ship.
rule.rationale: Ready items beyond 2-sprint capacity will be re-refined when they finally reach sprint planning, making the earlier work waste.

rule.statement: Tag every item with exactly one type: feature, bug, tech_debt, or research. Mixed-type backlogs cannot be reasoned about or correctly prioritized.
rule.rationale: Type tags are required for capacity allocation decisions (ratio of feature to tech debt), health metrics, and agent-based triage.

rule.statement: Source-link every item to its origin (ticket URL, customer quote, dashboard chart). Items without a source are archive candidates at the next grooming.
rule.rationale: Sourceless items cannot be validated or traced back when stakeholders ask "why did we build this?"

example.note: Item: "Add autocomplete to product search — As a customer I want to see suggestions as I type so I find products faster. Given I type 2 characters, when I pause, then I see top 5 suggestions. Estimate: M. Source: support-ticket-4521."
Item: "Better search — Search doesn't work well." No story, no AC, no source, no estimate.
